name: INEZ
narrative: Hurricane Inez exploded onto the scene over the open Atlantic on September 21, 1966, destined to become a monster. The system tracked westward during its lifetime. Its track spanned the open Atlantic, the central Atlantic, the Caribbean Sea, the western Atlantic. The storm covered a vast area, spanning roughly 16 degrees of latitude. It rapidly intensified into a Category 5 hurricane on September 28, with maximum sustained winds reaching 161 mph (140 kt). The minimum central pressure bottomed out at 927 mb, a testament to the storm's ferocity. It struck the Caribbean Sea on September 27 as a Category 3 hurricane with 126 mph winds. It made landfall near the Caribbean Sea on September 29 as a Category 5 hurricane with 161 mph winds. The storm made 7 additional landfalls before finally weakening. Sapped of its energy, the system wound down to a tropical depression and dissipated. Remarkably, the system churned on for 20 days — an exceptionally long-lived cyclone. It dissipated over the open Atlantic on October 11. narrative: What would become a devastating major hurricane first appeared as a tropical disturbance over the Caribbean on October 21, 2012 — Hurricane Sandy was born. It tracked northward across the basin. Its track spanned the Caribbean Sea, the western Atlantic, the western Atlantic off the U.S. East Coast. As it raced into higher latitudes, the storm shed its tropical identity and became extratropical. The storm persisted for 10 days, making it notably long-lived. The storm finally wound down over the western Atlantic off the U.S. East Coast on October 31. Superstorm Sandy merged with a powerful nor'easter to become an unprecedented hybrid storm that devastated the New Jersey and New York coastlines with a record storm surge. The massive cyclone — nearly 1,000 miles across — killed 233 people and caused $70 billion in damage. Sandy's 13-foot surge flooded lower Manhattan, shutting down the New York subway system and plunging much of the city into darkness. It carved a classic recurving track — west through the Caribbean, then curling northeastward. Its track spanned the central Atlantic, the Caribbean Sea, the western Atlantic, the Gulf of Mexico. It reached peak winds of 63 mph (55 kt) on August 16. The minimum central pressure was recorded at 991 mb. It carved a destructive path into the Caribbean Sea on August 11 with 46 mph winds. It slammed into the northern Gulf Coast on August 16 with 63 mph winds. The storm persisted for 10 days, making it notably long-lived. It dissipated over the western Atlantic off the U.S. East Coast on August 20.

name: GRACE

name: IDA
narrative: On August 26, 2021, the 2021 hurricane season produced one of its fiercest storms: Hurricane Ida formed over the Caribbean. The storm followed the textbook recurvature pattern, sweeping west before arcing into the mid-latitudes. Its track spanned the Caribbean Sea, the Gulf of Mexico, the open Atlantic, the western Atlantic off the U.S. East Coast. The storm covered a vast area, spanning roughly 32 degrees of latitude. Ida's remnants then triggered catastrophic flash flooding in the northeastern U.S., killing 91 people from Louisiana to New York.
